lessons.title: COMPREHENSION
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Comprehension is a process of active engagement that leads to accurate
understanding and interpretation of what is heard or read. The importance of
comprehension lies in its application to real-life situations such as understanding
instructions, engaging in conversations, and interpreting information. Learners will read
activities: information or texts:
 Ask learners to skim through the passage to get the general idea
 Time learners to ensure that they read at a speed of 200 words per minute.
 Guide learners to avoid bad reading habits
Stress is the body’s natural, human response to challenges or demands. It is a

situations. Many different situations can cause stress, and this can change
depending on our age. For instance, stress in teenagers may be caused by
assessment:  Learners discuss the main points of the passage in pairs.
 Use the question-and-answer technique to lead a discussion of the new vocabulary
in the passage.
 Learners comprehensively answer questions based on the passage
 Intensive Reading is the ability to read with understanding.

lessons.title: NARRATIVES
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Narrative writing is a type of composition that tells a story with a clear
beginning, middle and end. It involves presenting a sequence of events, either real or
imagined, in a way that engages the reader. It usually has characters who drive the action.
Narratives can be written in different ways with characters as the main movers of the
activities:  Ask Learners to read a given story (Teacher can also read the story aloud to the class)
Sample story
A Bridge for Nabwalya Village
In Nabwalya village of the Muchinga escarpment, there was a river that divided
the land. For many years, the villagers struggled to cross it, using canoes that
were often swept away by strong currents.
In the village was a young woman named Mulenga. She was not a village elder
and she did not have any position of leadership. Mulenga saw the struggles of
assessment:  Story writing involves telling a real or imaginary story
 When writing short stories, there is need to follow a clear layout (beginning, middle and
 A story needs to have a title, plot, characters, setting, theme, conflict, style (elements of a
 It is possible to write stories based on given situations (completing story or writing from
a question,

lessons.title: RESULT
topics: 0 INTRODUCTION
activities: Type 1: Using Therefore, as a result, for this reason, consequently, to express result
 Ask a tall learner to come to the board and touch the top of the board.
 Then write:
Lombe is tall. Therefore, she can touch the top of the board.
 Explain that in this type of expression of result, two sentences are used.
e.g. The bridge was swept away. Therefore, Mwila could not cross the river.
As a result,
For this reason,
assessment: Ask learners to pick sentences from column A and join with one from column B. Use any of the
words learnt to express result.
The boys were found using drugs Our ice cream melted
There was too much Gender based violence in
the country
She collapsed at the office

lessons.title: SUMMARY WRITING
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: A summary is a concise and brief retelling of the main points and key details
from a larger piece of content, such as an article, book, movie or speech. It extracts the
essential information into a shorter format while preserving the core message or
storyline. Summarising is a vital skill in both academic and everyday settings. It allows
activities:  Ask learners to read a given passage.
Too many challenges in one’s life affect mental health. Your overall well-
being, how you think, feel and handle life can show the state of your mental
health. Mental health care should be a priority in anyone’s life. It is all about,
consistent efforts that strengthen resilience and help you feel at ease. Making
self-care a priority, staying active, connecting with other people helps you to
improve your mental health. Setting realistic goals for yourself and seeking
professional help when you need it can also help.
assessment: Ask learners to read given passages and provide suitable titles.
 A good title gives a clear idea of what a piece of writing contains, yet it uses very
few words.
 It is supposed to be clear, brief and relevant.
CHAPTER 2: NATIONAL VALUES AND PRINCIPLES
LISTENING AND SPEAKING

lessons.title: DESCRIPTIVE WRITING
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Descriptive writing is an essential skill in both personal and professional
contexts, as it allows individuals to vividly convey experiences, emotions, and
observations to others. In real life, effective descriptive writing helps people
communicate more clearly and persuasively, whether it is in creative writing,
activities:  Learners read the passage.
A Description of a Monkey
A monkey is a lively creature, full of energy. Its fur is a soft blanket of golden-brown,
with a lighter cream colour on its belly. Like a crown, its fur glows in the sunlight,
shimmering as it moves. Its face is full of character: large, dark eyes that sparkle with
curiosity, a small, flat nose, and a mischievous smile that never fades.
The monkey’s hands are like tiny, delicate claws, quick and nimble. It swings through the
trees with the grace of a dancer, leaping from branch to branch, its long tail curling like
assessment:  Learners choose an animal (either familiar or exotic) and write a detailed description
focusing on sensory details like appearance, behaviour, and habitat
 Learners write short descriptions on given topics (Describe including physical traits,
sounds, movements, and personal impressions to engage the reader).
 Descriptive writing helps convey experiences, emotions, and observations clearly and
vividly.

lessons.title: ADVERBS
topics: Introduction
 Overview: Adverbs are words that modify or give more information about verbs,
adjectives, or other adverbs in a sentence. Understanding the position of adverbs is
crucial in constructing clear and accurate sentences. This topic will explore the different
positions where adverbs can appear within a sentence, such as before or after the main
activities:  Introduce and explain the different kinds of adverbs: adverbs of place, time,
frequency, manner, and degree.
Adverbs of place answer the question "Where?" (e.g., here, there,
everywhere).
Adverbs of time answer the question "When?" (e.g., now, later,
Adverbs of frequency answer the question "How often?" (e.g.,
always, often, never).
Adverbs of manner answer the question "How?" (e.g., quickly,
assessment:  Observation: Teacher observes learners as they classify adverbs and
provide examples, checking for understanding of each adverb's type
(place, time, frequency, manner, degree).
 Questioning: Ask learners to give additional examples of each type of
adverb and explain their function in the sentence. This will help gauge
whether they can identify adverbs correctly.

lessons.title: ADJECTIVES
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Adjectives are words used to describe or modify nouns and pronouns. They
help add detail, emotion and colour to language, making speech and writing more vivid
and precise. Adjectives answer questions like what kind, how many, and which one.
Understanding how to use adjectives correctly can greatly improve one’s ability to
activities:  Learners identify the adjectives in sentences.
Examples:
i. He is a kind handsome young man.
ii. She wore a nice red dress.
iii. I saw a cute little white cat on my way home.
 Explain to the learners that there is need to follow an acceptable sequence when more
than one adjective is describing a noun.
 Explain that the position of the adjective depends on how closely it is related in idea to
assessment:  Observe as the learners practice constructing sentences.
 Learners rearrange the order of adjectives in sentences.
 Individually, learners construct sentences with the correct order of adjectives.
 The order of adjectives refers to the specific sequence in which multiple adjectives are
arranged before a noun.
 Adjectives are words we can use to make our writing more vivid.

lessons.title: INTENSIFIERS
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: An intensifier is a word that strengthens or weakens or give emphasis to
another word. The purpose of an intensifier is to tell us the intensity of another word.
activities:  Give learners three sentences which have intensifiers
Example:
Joyce is incredibly smart
Chilombu is extremely happy.
This meal is absolutely delicious
I am utterly exhausted.
 Underline the intensifiers in the sentences
 Ask the learners what role the words are doing in the sentences.
assessment:  Observe learners using intensifiers in sentences.
 Learners identify and highlight intensifiers in a piece of writing such as a magazine,
newspaper, or text.
 Use intensifiers sparingly to avoid over emphasising or sounding insincere.
 Choose the right intensifier to convey the intended degree of emphasis.
 By using intensifiers, you can add appropriate depth and emphasis to your language

lessons.title: INTRODUCTION TO SUMMARY
topics: Introduction
Overview: A summary is a concise and clear restatement of the main ideas or
points from a larger piece of text. It distils essential information while omitting
unnecessary details, allowing readers to grasp the core meaning in a shorter form.
Summarising can be done in various ways, and one of the most common methods
activities:  Provide a paragraph to the learners for reading.
In recent years, the global community has witnessed an increasing shift
toward renewable energy sources. As the harmful effects of fossil fuels
become more evident, countries are looking for sustainable alternatives.
Solar power, wind energy, and hydropower are leading the way, with
advancements in technology making these sources more efficient and cost-
effective. Governments around the world are investing in green energy
projects, and many companies are beginning to transition to more eco-
resources:  Lead the discussion by asking questions about the stories and information read.
What lesson(s) do we get from the story?
 Learners share by retelling stories or explaining the information read. (Stories could be
assessment:  Ask learners to pair up and share their sentence summaries with each other.
 Encourage learners to give constructive feedback on how well the sentence
captures the core message of the paragraph.
 Encourage learners to practise writing sentence summaries for other passages as a
way of improving their comprehension and synthesis skills.

 Overview: Comprehension is a process of active engagement that leads to accurate

lessons.title: EXPOSITORY WRITING
topics: INTRODUCTION
Expository writing is a type of writing that explains, describes, or informs. It is different from
narrative or persuasive writing because it focuses on giving clear and factual information
rather than telling a story or convincing someone. Good expository writing answers questions
such as:
activities: research
 Explain to the learners that:
It helps one develop critical thinking
Allows Learners to organise ideas logically and express themselves effectively
using facts and explanations
It is important for gathering and sharing information, and research
Helps readers grasp complex ideas
It enhances the development of communication skills
resources:  Learners discuss the features of an expository essay. The following are the expected
Title: The title should reflect the topic being discussed and indicate the purpose of the
essay. For example, “The Effects of Climate Change on the Environment.”
assessment:  Group learners and give them an expository composition and observe them as they
identify features of an essay (topic sentence, supporting details and transition words)
 Individual work: ask learners to choose a simple topic and write a short expository essay
(e.g., How to Stay Healthy or The Importance of Education)
 Peer review: learners exchange essays and give feedback for clarity and organisation.
 Expository writing focuses on explaining, informing, or describing a topic clearly and

lessons.title: QUESTION TAGS
topics: INTRODUCTION
Good communication is essential in both spoken and written language. One way we make
conversations more engaging and interactive is by using question tags. These are short
questions added to the end of statements to confirm information, seek agreement, or
encourage a response.
activities:  Write sentences on the board and ask learners what they notice.
Example:
i. You love social media, don’t you?
ii. He hasn’t posted on Instagram today, has he?
 Explain what question tags are and the rules.
Questions tags are short questions added at the end of a statement to confirm or check
information. They help keep information or seek agreement from the listener.
If the main sentence is positive, the tag is negative. E.g. She is online, isn’t she?
assessment:  Observe learners’ participation and their oral responses (ability to explain).
 Carry out peer discussions for learners to correct each other’s mistakes.
 Let each learner write one correct question tag sentence.
 Question tags are short questions added at the end of statements to confirm information
or seek agreement.
 Positive statements take negative tags (“You use Google, don’t you?”).

lessons.title: SUMMARY
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: A summary is a brief and concise piece of writing that presents only
main points from a given text (oral or written) without altering the meaning of the
text. The ability to summarise a text is an important skill in academic and social
contexts in which there is endless need to make information easier to
activities:  Display a chart with a list of words and ask learners to provide abbreviations for
Examples:
Doctor – Dr.
Mister – Mr.
Avenue – Av.
February – Feb.
Kilogram - Kg
Minute – Min
assessment:  Observe learners as they discuss and practice writing activities.
 Assess individual understanding and correct usage of abbreviations.
 Ask learners to write a short paragraph with at least five different abbreviations
 Abbreviations are shortened forms of words or phrases used to save time and space.
 There are different types of abbreviations (Initialisms, Acronyms, shortened words,
Date and Time).
